items.q: What property of a sound wave determines its pitch?
items.correct: The frequency — higher frequency means higher pitch.
items.misconception: Amplitude determines pitch — a bigger wave is a higher-pitched note.
items.random_wrong: The speed of the wave determines pitch — faster waves sound higher.
items.why: Students conflate the two main wave properties: amplitude relates to volume, frequency to pitch, but they often swap them.

items.q: Is radioactive decay periodic — does each nucleus decay at regular intervals?
items.correct: No — decay is a random process; half-life is a statistical property of a large population.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus 'ticks' at intervals of one half-life until it decays.
items.random_wrong: Yes — half-life is the time between each emission from the source.
items.why: Students treat half-life as a clock for individual atoms instead of a probability-based property of bulk samples.

items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.
The answer examiners want is: Cannot be squashed. “Are easily squashed” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Stormy weather. “Sunny weather” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Water level rises from 50 cm³ to 78 cm³ when a stone is dropped in. What is the stone's volume in cm³?
items.why: The real figure is 28. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
